title: Angkor Wat Stories in the Bas-Reliefs: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk and Depictions of Devis - Angkor Grace
highlights: Stories in the Bas-Reliefs: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk and Depictions of Devis Angkor Wat Stories, One of the most fascinating and symbolic stories depicted in the bas-reliefs of Angkor Wat is the Churning of the Ocean of Milk. This story, drawn from Hindu mythology, is a cosmic tale of the gods (Devas) and demons (Asuras) who, in a fierce battle for immortality, churn the ocean to extract the amrita—the elixir of life. In the Angkor Wat bas-reliefs, this scene comes to life with great detail. The gods and demons are shown pulling a massive serpent, Vasuki, wrapped around Mount Mandara, using it as a rope to churn the ocean. As the churning progresses, it releases not only the precious nectar but also a host of divine treasures, including the goddess  ... title: Churning of the Ocean of Milk | Angkor Wat, Cambodia | Attractions
highlights:
the most famous of the bas-relief scenes at Angkor Wat, the Churning of the Ocean of Milk. This brilliantly executed carving depicts 88 asuras on the left, and 92 devas, with crested helmets, churning up the sea to extract from it the elixir of immortality. The demons hold the head of the serpent Vasuki and the gods hold its tail. At the centre of the sea, Vasuki is coiled around Mt Mandala, which turns and churns up the water in the tug of war between the demons and the gods.
